county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Bayamón, established 1772, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bayamón, founded in 1772, is Puerto Rico's second-largest city and part of the San Juan metropolitan area. Parroquia Santa Cruz has extensive records from the 18th century. As parent to Cataño (1927) and co-parent to several other municipios, Bayamón records cover a wide population base. The sugar industry dominated until the 20th century — Hacienda Santa Ana records at AGPR are particularly valuable. Rapid urbanization from the 1950s under Operation Bootstrap brought massive population changes. The Archivo Histórico de Bayamón supplements AGPR holdings. Cattle ranching (hatos) preceded sugar — early land grants document this transition period.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Bayamón municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1772.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Bayamón / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Bayamón including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Bayamón district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1772. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Bayamón. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Bayamón district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Bayamón district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Bayamón / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Bayamón. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Bayamón including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

source_profiles.source_name: Bayamón Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Colección Puertorriqueña, UPR / Biblioteca Nacional / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers published in or covering Bayamón. Puerto Rico's newspaper tradition began in the early 19th century. Papers include birth/death/marriage announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news. La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette) published government notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some newspaper runs are incomplete. The Colección Puertorriqueña at UPR Río Piedras has the most extensive holdings. Chronicling America has digitized selected Puerto Rico newspapers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Cabo Rojo, established 1771, carved from San Germán. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cabo Rojo, separated from San Germán in 1771, is the southwestern corner of Puerto Rico. Birthplace of Ramón Emeterio Betances (1827), father of Puerto Rican independence. Parroquia San Miguel Arcángel holds records from the 1770s. The salt flats (Salinas de Cabo Rojo) and fishing economy created distinctive occupational records. Corsican, French, and Venezuelan immigrants contributed to the population in the early 19th century — passenger arrival records and naturalization documents at AGPR are valuable. Pre-1771 records are in San Germán, one of PR's two original settlements. Lighthouse (Faro Los Morrillos) area records document coastal settlement.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Caguas, established 1775, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Caguas, established as a parish in 1775, is a major inland city in the Turabo Valley east of San Juan. Known as 'La Ciudad Criolla,' it has rich genealogical resources. Parroquia Dulce Nombre de Jesús holds baptism, marriage, and burial records from the late 18th century. As parent to Aguas Buenas (1838), Gurabo (1815), and co-parent to San Lorenzo, Caguas records serve a wide eastern interior population. The tobacco industry generated hacienda records and merchant ledgers at AGPR. Centro de Investigaciones Históricas at UPR holds related manuscript collections. Sugar cultivation along the Río Turabo also produced enslaved persons records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Camuy, established 1807, carved from Arecibo, Hatillo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Camuy was carved from Arecibo and Hatillo in 1807 on the north coast. Parroquia San José holds records from the early 19th century. The Río Camuy cave system (Cavernas del Río Camuy) area barrios have distinctive settlement patterns. Pre-1807 records are in Arecibo's parish archives. Sugar and cattle raising dominated the colonial economy — hacienda records at AGPR document landholders and workers. Camuy served as parent to Quebradillas (1823), so researchers should trace back through this lineage. The town's location between Arecibo and Aguadilla made it a transit point — check military and postal records for family connections.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Canóvanas. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Canóvanas, established 1909, carved from Loíza. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Canóvanas, one of Puerto Rico's newest municipios, was carved from Loíza in 1909. Located east of San Juan in the Río Grande de Loíza valley. Parroquia del Santísimo Sacramento holds records from the early 20th century — pre-1909 records are in Loíza, which has some of Puerto Rico's oldest and most important Afro-Puerto Rican genealogical records. The former Hacienda El Comandante area (now a racetrack) documents the sugar economy. Hurricane María (2017) caused significant damage to local records and infrastructure. Check Loíza parish registers extensively for pre-1909 family data. Civil registry records from 1909 are at the Registro Demográfico.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Carolina, established 1857, carved from Trujillo Alto. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carolina was separated from Trujillo Alto in 1857 and lies east of San Juan, now a major metropolitan suburb. Named for King Carlos II, it hosts Luis Muñoz Marín International Airport. Parroquia San Fernando records begin in the 1850s. Pre-1857 records are in Trujillo Alto and ultimately Bayamón. Rapid urbanization from the 1950s transformed a sugar-producing area into suburban development — property records at the Registro de la Propiedad document this transition. Isla Verde beach area was a separate barrio with distinct records. Check US military records at NARA for the former Buchanan Army Airfield (WWII era) and associated personnel.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Cataño, established 1927, carved from Bayamón. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cataño, carved from Bayamón in 1927, is Puerto Rico's smallest municipio, directly across San Juan Bay. Home to the Bacardí rum distillery (founded 1862 in Cuba, moved to PR 1936). Parroquia Inmaculada Concepción holds records from 1927 — all earlier records are in Bayamón's parish and municipal archives. As the newest municipio on the main island, civil registry and municipal records begin late. The ferry (lancha) between Cataño and Old San Juan has operated for centuries — port and transportation records provide genealogical context. Industrial development in the 20th century brought workers from across the island — check employer records and union membership lists.

source_profiles.source_name: Cataño Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Cataño municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1927.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1927. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Cataño district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1927. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Cataño US Federal Census Records (1910-1950)
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: US federal census records for Cataño from 1927. Puerto Rico was included in US census enumerations from 1910. Pre-formation census data for this area is found under parent municipio(s). The 1910-1940 census records are accessible on FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: US federal census records for Puerto Rico are generally complete from 1910. The 1950 census is the most recent publicly available.

source_profiles.source_name: Cataño Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Cataño district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Cataño district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Cataño Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Cataño / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Cataño. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Cayey, established 1773, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cayey, established as a parish around 1773, sits in the Sierra de Cayey mountains between San Juan and Ponce. Home to a University of Puerto Rico campus. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Asunción holds records from the late 18th century. As parent to Cidra (1809), pre-1809 records for Cidra are here. The Cayey military reservation (Henry Barracks/Camp Henry) from the US period (1898-1960s) created military records at NARA. Tobacco cultivation was the primary economy — check hacienda records at AGPR. The Ruta Panorámica mountain pass area has distinct barrio settlement histories from the colonial period.

source_profiles.source_name: Cayey Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Cayey municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1773.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Cayey Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Cayey. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Ceiba, established 1838, carved from Fajardo, Naguabo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ceiba was carved from Fajardo and Naguabo in 1838 on the eastern coast. Parroquia San Antonio de Padua holds records from the mid-19th century. The former Roosevelt Roads Naval Station (1943-2004) dominated the area for decades — military and civilian employee records at NARA are essential for 20th-century research. Pre-1838 records split between Fajardo and Naguabo parishes. Sugar plantations and later the US Navy created distinct population layers. The base closure in 2004 led to significant demographic change. Check both Fajardo and Naguabo church records for pre-separation families, and naval base personnel records for the modern period.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Ciales, established 1820, carved from Morovis. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ciales was separated from Morovis in 1820 in the north-central mountain region. Known for its coffee production and the legend of the Taíno cacique Ciales. Parroquia San Miguel holds records from the 1820s. Pre-1820 records are in Morovis and ultimately in Manatí and Toa Alta. Coffee hacienda records at AGPR are primary sources for the agricultural economy. The 1868 Grito de Lares rebellion involved Ciales residents — check military tribunal records and political prisoner lists at AGPR and Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid. Barrios like Cordillera, Fronton, and Pesas have distinctive mountain settlement histories.
